title: Tamerlane's World
bullets: Timur was born around 1336 near Kesh in modern Uzbekistan into the Barlas, a minor Turco-Mongol clan inside the fragmenting Chagatai Khanate, and he was decidedly not in the senior Genghisid line — a fact that would constrain him for the rest of his life.

By the 1340s the Chagatai Khanate had split into a settled western half and a nomadic eastern half called Moghulistan, with real power held by competing tribal emirs while puppet Chagatai khans served as figureheads — the political vacuum Timur would exploit.
A wound in Sistan around 1363 left Timur permanently lame in his right leg and weakened in his right arm, giving him the Persian nickname Timur-i Lang and the European corruption Tamerlane.

The Soviet anthropologist Mikhail Gerasimov opened Timur's tomb at the Gur-e-Amir mausoleum on June 19, 1941; the skeleton confirmed every detail of the chronicle accounts of his lameness, and Nazi Germany invaded the Soviet Union three days later.
Timur captured Samarkand in 1370 after eliminating his brother-in-law and former partner Amir Husayn, and held a kurultai at which he was acknowledged as sovereign of Transoxiana at age thirty-four.

Because Timur was not a direct descendant of Genghis Khan, the steppe Yasa code forbade him from using the title Khan — so he ruled through Genghisid puppet khans named Suyurghatmish and Mahmud for his entire thirty-five-year reign.

His marriage to Saray Mulk Khanum, a Chagatayid princess descended from Genghis Khan, gave him the title Guregen ('royal son-in-law'), which became his most-used official title alongside the modest Amir.

Timurid court historians eventually invented elaborate forged genealogies linking Timur's family to a putative shared Mongol ancestor with Genghis Khan — fabrications that the Mughal court would later inherit and elaborate further.
subtitle: a lame minor warlord captures Samarkand and works around the Genghisid legitimacy gap

title: The Conqueror's Method
bullets: Timur's army was a Mongol-style cavalry confederation augmented by integrated infantry and siege engineering teams, capable of conducting campaigns across thousands of miles in a single season — the last great pre-gunpowder imperial military system.

His standard policy after a city resisted was a population massacre followed by stacking the heads of the dead into pyramidal towers near the city's gates — a deliberate signaling device documented at Sabzevar, Isfahan, Tikrit, Aleppo, Baghdad, and Damascus.
The Isfahan massacre of 1387 killed perhaps 70,000 people after the city revolted against a Timurid tax-collection unit, and the resulting skull pyramid became the model for what followed.

After every sack Timur deported the surviving skilled craftsmen — weavers, calligraphers, miniature painters, masons, mathematicians — to Samarkand to work on his building projects, concentrating the artistic capital of the Islamic world by force.

Tokhtamysh, the Genghisid Golden Horde khan whom Timur had personally helped install on his throne, turned against his patron in the late 1380s and was crushed in two devastating Timurid campaigns into the Russian steppe in 1391 and 1395.

The destruction of the Golden Horde at the Kondurcha River in 1391 and the Terek River in 1395 inadvertently freed the Russian principalities — particularly Moscow — from Mongol tribute pressure and enabled the rise of a unified Russian state under Ivan III in the 1480s.

Timur's empire was a raid-and-loot system at imperial scale, taking enormous wealth but devastating the agricultural infrastructure of every region it conquered — some Iranian provinces did not return to their pre-Timur populations for centuries.

The cultural florescence at fifteenth-century Herat that produced the Bihzad miniature tradition rested on craft capacity that had been built through deportation across thirty years of systematic terror — there is no clean separation between the cultural achievement and the violence that funded it.
subtitle: the skull pyramids, the Tokhtamysh wars, and the deportation infrastructure that built Samarkand

title: Persia and the Caliphal Heartland
bullets: When the Ilkhanate collapsed in 1335 the Iranian and Iraqi territories fragmented into competing successor states — the Jalayirids, the Muzaffarids, the Sarbadars, the Karts of Herat — none of them strong enough individually to resist Timur and unable to coordinate against him.

Through 1381–1387 Timur conquered Khorasan and the Caspian provinces; through 1386–1394 he took central and southern Iran; the Isfahan massacre of 1387 produced a skull pyramid that contemporary Persian chroniclers described in horrified detail.

The first sack of Baghdad in 1393 was relatively restrained because the Jalayirid sultan Ahmad fled and the city surrendered — Timur installed an Abbasid Sayyid puppet caliph as a piece of religious-legitimacy theater parallel to his Genghisid puppet-khan arrangement on the steppe.

The second sack of Baghdad in 1401 was the catastrophic one: each Timurid soldier was ordered to bring at least two human heads, the resulting skull pyramid was one of the largest Timur ever built, and the city's population fell to perhaps 20,000 from a previous level of 100,000 to 150,000.

Combined with the original Mongol sack of 1258, the 1401 destruction reduced Baghdad from a recovering former imperial capital to a regional town with no political weight for several centuries — an inflection point in the long decline of Arab-Islamic intellectual primacy.

The center of gravity of Islamic intellectual culture shifted permanently after the Timurid sacks: eastward to Persianate centers eventually culminating in Safavid Isfahan, westward to Mamluk Cairo, and to Ottoman Istanbul after 1453.

The deportation policy systematically concentrated craft and intellectual labor at Samarkand — Tabriz lost its miniature painters, Shiraz lost its calligraphers, Damascus lost its weavers, Baghdad lost its scholarly community after 1401.

Timur's 1401 meeting with the historian Ibn Khaldun outside besieged Damascus is one of the best primary-source descriptions of his intellect; Ibn Khaldun was struck by how sophisticated and politically curious the conqueror actually was beneath the brutality.
subtitle: the Persian conquest, two sacks of Baghdad, and the long damage to the Arab intellectual world

title: India and Anatolia
bullets: Timur's 1398 march from Samarkand to Delhi crossed roughly 1,500 miles in a single campaign cycle — over the Hindu Kush, across the Indus, through Punjab — and was one of the most logistically ambitious operations of the medieval world.

At the Battle of Delhi on December 17, 1398, Timur's cavalry broke the Tughlaq war elephant corps with burning camels, and in the chaos that followed Delhi was sacked so completely that contemporary accounts describe it uninhabitable for years afterward.

The Tughlaq dynasty effectively ended in 1398 even though nominal Tughlaq sultans continued briefly; the long arc of Sultanate decay from 1398 through the Sayyid and Lodi successor dynasties ended only when Babur took Delhi at Panipat in 1526.

Timur sacked Aleppo in 1400 with the standard skull pyramid, then besieged and took Damascus in 1401 — the Damascus weavers were deported to Samarkand and the Umayyad Mosque suffered fire damage during the sack.

The Battle of Ankara on July 28, 1402, captured the Ottoman sultan Bayezid I when his Anatolian Turkish vassal contingents defected to Timur's side and turned on their former comrades — the first time a major Ottoman sultan had been captured in battle.

Bayezid spent eight months in Timurid custody and died on March 8, 1403, probably of illness — the famous story of the iron cage and the naked wife serving at banquets is largely Marlowe's 1587 dramatization rather than the historical record.

Bayezid's death triggered the Ottoman Interregnum of 1402–1413, in which his sons fought a multi-sided civil war that nearly destroyed the empire — Constantinople did not fall until 1453, half a century after the defeat at Ankara.

The Ottoman defeat at Ankara was the last great victory of the Mongol-style cavalry confederation over the early gunpowder-age standing army — twelve years later Ottoman descendants of Bayezid would defeat the Safavids at Chaldiran with the very system Timur had broken at Ankara.
subtitle: the Delhi sack, the Damascus skull pyramid, and the captured Ottoman sultan

title: The Death and the Will
bullets: In late 1404 Timur assembled an army of perhaps 200,000 at Samarkand for the invasion of Ming China, framed as a holy war to convert China to Islam but actually a punitive campaign against the Yongle Emperor for a 1395 diplomatic insult.

The Yongle Emperor in Beijing was preparing defenses on the Gansu frontier; the Great Wall sections in the affected sectors were reinforced through 1404 — the largest planned military operation of the fifteenth century was waiting to happen.
Timur died at Otrar on the Syr Darya on February 17 or 18, 1405, probably of a respiratory infection contracted in the freezing winter march — the China invasion ended before it began.

His tomb at the Gur-e-Amir mausoleum in Samarkand established the architectural template — high turquoise drum dome on a square chamber with banna'i tilework — that would define the Persianate world for the next two centuries and culminate in the Taj Mahal.

The succession war that followed lasted four years and killed several major contenders: Khalil Sultan seized Samarkand and emptied the treasury on his Persian wife, Pir Muhammad was assassinated in 1407, Miran Shah was killed in 1408 fighting the Kara Qoyunlu.

Shah Rukh, Timur's fourth son, finally moved from his Khorasan governorship in 1409, defeated Khalil Sultan, captured but did not execute his nephew, and consolidated control of the reduced Timurid heartland — Khorasan, Transoxiana, parts of central Persia.

The empire that emerged from the four-year succession war had lost Mesopotamia, western Persia, the Caucasus, Anatolia, and India — about half of Timur's peak territory — but the surviving core was politically more sustainable.

Shah Rukh's strategic decision to consolidate rather than expand was a fundamental reorientation of the Timurid project from a Mongol-style war-making machine into a settled Persianate state, and it produced the cultural florescence at Herat that defined the dynasty's lasting legacy.
subtitle: the planned China invasion, the death at Otrar, and Shah Rukh's consolidation

title: Shah Rukh and the Herat Renaissance
bullets: Shah Rukh moved the political capital from Samarkand to Herat — a deeply Persianate Khorasani city — and the move marked the decisive turn from the empire's Mongol-Turkic founding identity toward its Persianate cultural identity.

Gawhar Shad, Shah Rukh's principal wife, controlled independent financial resources, governed Mashhad, commanded household troops, and personally directed the most ambitious architectural patronage of the Timurid period — the conventional histories systematically underplay her role.

The Gawhar Shad Mosque at Mashhad, completed in 1418, attached the great shrine of Imam Reza and survives largely intact today as the most important Shia pilgrimage destination in Iran outside the Iraqi shrine cities.

Most of the Gawhar Shad Musalla complex at Herat was deliberately demolished in 1885 by British and Russian advisors of the Afghan Amir, who wanted clear sight lines for Herat's defenses against a feared Russian invasion — one of the great architectural losses of the modern period.

The Timurid banna'i tilework technique — large glazed and unglazed bricks laid in geometric patterns — allowed monumental buildings to be covered with ornament much faster than full mosaic and became the visual signature inherited intact by both Safavid and Mughal architecture.

The Herat school of miniature painting culminating in Bihzad's work in the 1480s and 1490s is the direct technical ancestor of all subsequent Persianate book art — every Safavid and Mughal painting tradition runs back to it.

Abd al-Rahman Jami of Herat is conventionally regarded as the last great classical Persian poet — a Naqshbandi Sufi who produced roughly sixty surviving works including the Haft Awrang ('Seven Thrones') poetic cycle.

Khwaja Ubaydullah Ahrar, the leading Naqshbandi sheikh of Samarkand from the 1450s through 1490, accumulated enormous estates and effectively controlled the city for decades — a model of independent religious authority that the political class proved unable to constrain.
subtitle: the move to Herat, Gawhar Shad's architecture, and the Persianate template the Mughals would inherit

title: Ulugh Beg the Astronomer-King
bullets: Ulugh Beg governed Samarkand for forty years from age fifteen and combined the duties of viceroy with a serious career as a working astronomer — an unprecedented combination in the history of pre-modern science.

The Samarkand observatory built between 1424 and 1428 was a three-story circular building whose principal instrument, a meridian arc with a radius of 130 feet, achieved an angular precision approaching one minute of arc — the highest accuracy anywhere in the world before telescopic measurement.
Jamshid al-Kashi, Ulugh Beg's senior mathematical advisor, calculated pi to sixteen decimal places, a precision unsurpassed for nearly two hundred years.

The Zij-i Sultani completed in 1437 included a star catalog of 1,018 stars with positions measured to one minute of arc — the most accurate star catalog produced anywhere in the world between Ptolemy in the second century and Tycho Brahe in the 1580s.

Ulugh Beg's son Abdal-Latif had his father beheaded outside Samarkand on October 27, 1449, on hasty charges of un-Islamic occultism — the parricide horrified contemporary opinion and Abdal-Latif was killed by his own troops six months later.

The Samarkand observatory was abandoned within months of Ulugh Beg's death, the team of astronomers dispersed, and the building eventually destroyed by an earthquake — its location was forgotten until the Russian archaeologist Vasily Vyatkin excavated the site in 1908.

Ali Qushji, the youngest member of the Samarkand astronomy team, migrated to Constantinople in 1471 and brought the latest Timurid astronomical methods to the Ottoman scientific establishment, where his proposal that the earth might rotate prefigured European Copernican discussions by decades.

The Samarkand observatory was the last major Islamic astronomical institution to operate at world-leading precision before the European telescopic revolution; its collapse meant three centuries before any Islamic-world institution achieved comparable astronomical accuracy again.
subtitle: the great Samarkand observatory, the Zij-i Sultani star catalog, and the parricide that ended it

title: The Long Twilight and Babur
bullets: After Sultan Abu Sa'id Mirza was killed by the Aq Qoyunlu in 1469 the Timurid Empire fragmented into rival principalities centered on Herat, Samarkand, and the small Ferghana Valley — none of them strong enough to resist the Uzbeks gathering in the north.

Sultan Husayn Bayqara ruled Herat for thirty-seven years until his death in 1506; his court hosted Jami the poet, Bihzad the painter, and Mir Ali Sher Nava'i — a cultural climax that exceeded even the earlier Shah Rukh-era achievements.

Mir Ali Sher Nava'i is conventionally regarded as the founder of Chagatai-Turkic literature; his Muhakamat al-Lughatayn argued that Chagatai-Turkic was a literary medium equal to Persian, an argument that shaped Turkic literary identity for centuries.
Muhammad Shaybani's Uzbeks captured Samarkand and Bukhara in 1500, took Herat in 1507, and consumed the rest of Khorasan by 1510 — the Timurid Empire ceased to exist as a sovereign state.

The dispersed Timurid cultural establishment scattered across the new political map: Bihzad to the Safavid court at Tabriz, other painters and scholars to the Mamluks, the Indian sultanates, and eventually the Mughal court — fifteenth-century Herat became the founding template for sixteenth-century Persianate civilization.

Babur, the last Timurid prince, was driven from Ferghana by his Uzbek cousins, captured Kabul in 1504, and made it his base for the next twenty-two years — the southward turn that took two decades to develop into the Mughal invasion.

At the First Battle of Panipat on April 21, 1526, Babur's 12,000 men defeated Ibrahim Lodi's force of perhaps 100,000 men and a thousand war elephants using two batteries of Ottoman field artillery and several hundred matchlock musketeers behind a wagon-fort line — Babur entered Delhi the same week and founded the Mughal Empire.

The Timurid dynasty had a continuous political existence — first in Central Asia, then in India under the Mughal name — from Timur's coronation at Samarkand in 1370 to the abolition of the Mughal title in 1858, an arc of 488 years that ranks with the longest dynastic continuities in early-modern world history.
subtitle: Husayn Bayqara's last cultural bloom, the Uzbek conquest, and Babur's flight to India
